content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 21, 1997


Daniel J. Russo
Russo, Weintraub, Bellia & Morton
521 Georgia Street
Vallejo, California 94590

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. A-97-144

Dear Mr. Russo:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. May campaign funds be used to reimburse a political official for fines assessed by the Fair Political Practices Commission?

2. What limits exist for fundraising events held to reimburse an individual for the cost of a fine assessed by the Fair Political Practices Commission?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. It is permissible to use campaign funds to pay a fine resulting from a conflicts of interest violation.

2. Section 85301 allows city council members in districts with fewer than 100,000 residents to accept contributions not totaling more than $100 from each individual. Such funds may be collected at any time to pay for fines for violations of the Act.

FACTS

Ms. Carey Corbaley is a member of the Benecia City Council and was its Vice Mayor. In

_______________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-144
Page 2

May and June 1994, she violated the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act by participating in
city council decisions in which she had a financial interest. Consequently, Vice Mayor Corbaley
agreed to the imposition of a fine of $3,500. She wishes to hold a fundraiser to reimburse herself
for such funds.

ANALYSIS

Using Campaign Funds for Personal Use

The personal use provisions of the Act govern the use of campaign funds. Where an
expenditure confers a substantial benefit on an officeholder, the expenditure must be directly
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.) The payment of an
officeholder's fine would confer a substantial personal benefit on a candidate.²

Section 89513 (c) provides that campaign funds may not be used to pay or reimburse
fines except in two specified circumstances. One of the permitted purposes is any action for
which payment of attorney's fees would be permitted by the Act. (Section 89513(c)(2).)

Section 89514 provides that campaign funds may be used to pay attorney's fees where the
litigation:

"Is directly related to activities of a committee that are consistent
with its primary objectives or arises directly out of a committee's
activities or out of a candidate's or elected officer's activities,
duties, or status as a candidate or elected officer, including, but not
limited to, an action to enjoin defamation, defense of an action
brought for a violation of state or local campaign disclosure, or
election laws, contest or recount."

In 1992, the Commission advised that an elected official could use campaign funds to defend
against a complaint alleging conflict-of-interest violations. (Lanning Advice Letter, No. A-92-
050.) As noted, an official may use campaign funds to pay any fine, if it would be permissible to
use campaign funds for attorney's fees for the same action. (Section 89513(c)(2); Johannessen
Advice Letter, No. A-96-281, copy enclosed.) Therefore, because it would be permissible to use
campaign funds to defend against the action, it is also permissible to use campaign funds to pay
the fine.

² The term substantial personal benefit is defined as "an expenditure of campaign funds which results in a direct
personal benefit with a value of more than $100 to a candidate, elected officer, or any individual or individuals with
authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee." (Section 89511(b)(3).) In the case
of a fine imposed on the candidate, payment with campaign funds would relieve the candidate of the obligation and
result in a direct personal benefit.

File No. A-97-144
Page 3

Contribution Limits and Off-Year Ban

A contribution is any payment made, among other things, for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. (Section 82015; Regulation 18215(a), copy enclosed.) A payment is made for political purposes if it is received by or made at the behest of a candidate. (Regulation 18215(a)(2); see section 82015 [providing that payments made to a candidate, as defined by section 82007, are presumed to be contributions].) Contributions to city council candidates, not subject to municipal spending limits, are governed by section 85301 because of the inability of such candidates to accept any voluntary expenditure ceilings. (Section 85400; emphasis added.) Section 85301 allows city council members in districts with fewer than 100,000 residents to accept contributions not totaling more than $100 from each individual.³ However, such contributions may not be accepted by a candidate more than six months prior to a primary or special primary election in which the candidate is attempting to be on the ballot or more than 90 days after the date of a candidates withdrawal, defeat, or election to office. (85305(a),(c).) Funds may be collected, however, at any time to pay for fines for violations of the Act.⁴ (Section 85305(d).)

As a city council member, you would be able to accept a contribution of $100 from each person to pay for your fine. Such funds may be collected at any time and are not subject to the prohibition against off-year fundraising. (Section 85305(d).) Thus, Vice Mayor Corbaley may immediately start raising funds, subject to section 85301 and the contribution limits therein.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Douglas White
Graduate Assistant, Legal Division

SGC:DW:ak

³ You stated that you are participating in a city council race. Therefore, this letter is based on the presumption that you are a candidate in a district with 100,000 or fewer residents. (Section 85301(a).)

⁴ Please note that in the Harmony Advice Letter, A-97-018, we advised that the blackout period applied to the collection of funds to pay for attorney's fees in connection with a defamation action.

1. May campaign funds be used to reimburse a political official for fines assessed by the 
Fair Political Practices Commission? 
2. What limits exist for fundraising events held to reimburse an individual for the cost of 
a fine assessed by the Fair Political Practices Commission?
